{{short description|21st-century English cultural theorist}}
{{Other people|Mark Fisher}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=January 2017}}
{{Infobox philosopher
| name          = Mark Fisher
| image         = Mark fisher.jpg
| alt           = 
| caption       = Fisher in 2011
| birth_name    = 
| other_names   = k-punk
| birth_date    = {{birth date|df=y|1968|7|11}}
| birth_place   = [[Leicester]], England
| death_date    = {{death date and age|df=y|2017|1|13|1968|7|11}}
| death_place   = [[Felixstowe]], England
| nationality   = 
| spouse        = Zoë
| children      = 1
| thesis_title  = Flatline Constructs
| thesis_url    = http://ethos.bl.uk/OrderDetails.do?uin=uk.bl.ethos.340547
| thesis_year   = 1999
| main_interests   = {{hlist | [[Critical theory]] | [[philosophy]] | [[music criticism]] | [[political theory]]}}
| school_tradition = [[Continental philosophy]]
| alma_mater    = {{ubl | [[University of Hull]] | [[University of Warwick]]}}
| institutions  = [[Goldsmiths, University of London|Goldsmiths' College, London]]
| notable_works = {{ubl | ''[[Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?|Capitalist Realism]]'' (2009) | ''k-punk'' blog (2003–2015) |
[[Exiting the Vampire Castle]]}}
| influences = {{hlist | [[Theodor Adorno]] | [[Jean Baudrillard]] | [[Franco Berardi]] | [[Gilles Deleuze]] | [[Jacques Derrida]] | [[Sigmund Freud]] | [[Antonio Gramsci]] | [[Félix Guattari]] | [[Fredric Jameson]] | [[Jacques Lacan]] | [[Nick Land]] | [[Karl Marx]] | [[Sadie Plant]] | [[Baruch Spinoza]] | [[Slavoj Žižek]]}}
| influenced = [[Nick Srnicek]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2017/may/11/accelerationism-how-a-fringe-philosophy-predicted-the-future-we-live-in|title=Accelerationism: How a fringe philosophy predicted the future we live in|website=[[TheGuardian.com]]|date=11 May 2017}}</ref>
| notable_ideas = [[Capitalist realism]], [[business ontology]]
| website       = {{URL|http://k-punk.abstractdynamics.org}}
}}

'''Mark Fisher''' (11 July 1968&nbsp;– 13 January 2017), also known under his blogging alias '''k-punk''', was an English writer, music critic, political and [[cultural theorist]], philosopher, and teacher based in the Department of Visual Cultures at [[Goldsmiths, University of London]]. He initially achieved acclaim for his [[blogging]] as k-punk in the early 2000s, and was known for his writing on [[radical politics]], [[music]], and [[popular culture]].

Fisher published several books, including the unexpected success ''[[Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?]]'' (2009), and contributed to publications such as ''[[The Wire (magazine)|The Wire]]'', ''[[Fact (UK magazine)|Fact]]'', ''[[New Statesman]]'' and ''[[Sight & Sound]]''. He was also the co-founder of [[Zero Books]], and later [[Repeater Books]]. He died by suicide in January 2017, shortly before the publication of ''The Weird and the Eerie'' (2017).

==Early life and education==
Fisher was born in [[Leicester]] and raised in [[Loughborough]] to [[Social class in the United Kingdom|working-class]], [[Conservatism in the United Kingdom|conservative]] parents; his father was an engineer and his mother a cleaner. He attended a local [[Comprehensive school (England and Wales)|comprehensive school]]. Fisher was formatively influenced in his youth by the [[post-punk]] music press of the late 1970s, particularly papers such as ''[[New Musical Express|NME]]'' which crossed music with politics, film, and fiction.<ref name="g"/> He was also influenced by the relationship between working class culture and football, being present at the [[Hillsborough disaster]].<ref>{{cite news |last=Niven |first=Alex |title=Mark Fisher, 1968-2017 |url=https://jacobinmag.com/2017/01/mark-fisher-capitalist-realism-vampire-castle |url-status=live |work=[[Jacobin (magazine)|Jacobin]] |date=19 January 2017 |access-date=28 January 2021}}</ref> Fisher earned a [[Bachelor of Arts]] degree in English and Philosophy at [[Hull University]] (1989), and completed a [[PhD]] at the [[University of Warwick]] in 1999 titled ''Flatline Constructs: Gothic Materialism and Cybernetic Theory-Fiction''.<ref>{{cite thesis |last=Fisher |first=Mark |degree=PhD |publisher=[[University of Warwick]] |title=Flatline constructs: Gothic materialism and cybernetic theory-fiction |url=http://ethos.bl.uk/OrderDetails.do?uin=uk.bl.ethos.340547 |date=1999 |website=ethos.bl.uk |id={{EThOS|uk.bl.ethos.340547}} |oclc=59534159 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101224015635/http://cinestatic.com/trans-mat/Fisher/FCcontents.htm |archive-date=2010-12-24}}</ref> During this time, Fisher was a founding member of the interdisciplinary collective known as the [[Cybernetic Culture Research Unit]], which were associated with [[accelerationist]] political thought and the work of philosophers [[Sadie Plant]] and [[Nick Land]].<ref name="g"/><ref>{{cite web |first=Mark |last=Fisher |title=Nick Land: Mind Games |url=http://www.dazeddigital.com/artsandculture/article/10459/1/nick-land-mind-games |work=Dazed |date=1 June 2011}}</ref> There, he befriended and influenced producer [[Kode9]], who would later found the [[Hyperdub]] record label.<ref name=wireobituary>{{cite web|title=Mark Fisher 1968–2017|website=[[The Wire (magazine)|The Wire]]|url= https://www.thewire.co.uk/news/45139/mark-fisher-1968-2017 |access-date=20 November 2018}}</ref> In the early 1990s, he also made music as part of the [[techno]] group D-Generation, releasing the 12" ''Entropy in the UK''.<ref name=wireobituary /><ref>{{cite news |last=Reynolds |first=Simon |title=D-Generation |url=https://reynoldsretro.blogspot.com/2018/11/d-generation-or-dawn-of-k-punk.html?m=1 |access-date=20 November 2018 |work=[[Melody Maker]]}}</ref>

After a period teaching in a further education college as a philosophy lecturer,<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Fisher, Mark |author2=Gilbert, Jeremy |title=Capitalist Realism and Neoliberal Hegemony: A Dialogue |journal=New Formations |issue=80–81 |date=Winter 2013 |pages=89–101 (at p. 90) |doi=10.3898/neWF.80/81.05.2013 |s2cid=142588084}}</ref> Fisher began his blog on cultural theory, ''k-punk'', in 2003.<ref name="Zero Books">{{Cite web|url=https://www.johnhuntpublishing.com/zer0-books/authors/mark-fisher|title=Mark Fisher|website=Zer0 Books}}</ref> Music critic [[Simon Reynolds]] described it as "a one-man magazine superior to most magazines in Britain"<ref name="g"/> and as the central hub of a "constellation of blogs" in which popular culture, music, film, politics, and [[critical theory]] were discussed in tandem by journalists, academics, and colleagues.<ref>[http://blog.frieze.com/music_theory/ frieze] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304073857/http://blog.frieze.com/music_theory/ |date=4 March 2016}}</ref> ''[[Vice (magazine)|Vice]]'' magazine later described his writing on ''k-punk'' as "lucid and revelatory, taking literature, music and cinema we're familiar with and effortlessly disclosing its inner secrets".<ref name="vice"/> Fisher used the blog as a more flexible, generative venue for writing, a respite from the frameworks and expectations of academic writing.<ref name="Jacobin-Aug2020-PopModernism">{{cite news |last=Braithwaite |first=Phoebe |title=Mark Fisher's Popular Modernism |url=https://jacobinmag.com/2020/08/mark-fishers-popular-modernism |work=[[Jacobin Magazine]] |date=11 August 2020}}</ref> Fisher also co-founded the message board Dissensus with writer Matt Ingram.<ref name="g">"[https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2017/jan/18/mark-fisher-k-punk-blogs-did-48-politics Mark Fisher's K-punk blogs were required reading for a generation]" by [[Simon Reynolds]], ''[[The Guardian]]'', 18 January 2017</ref>

His writing inspired the [[Omega]] and the notions of [[larping]] and [[gnosis]].[[Category:Omega]]

==Career==
Subsequently, Fisher was a visiting fellow and a lecturer on Aural and Visual Cultures at [[Goldsmiths College]], a commissioning editor at Zero books, an editorial board member of ''Interference: A Journal of Audio Culture'' and [[Edinburgh University Press]]'s [[Speculative Realism]] series, and an acting deputy editor at ''[[The Wire (magazine)|The Wire]]''.<ref name="gold">{{cite web|url=http://www.gold.ac.uk/visual-cultures/staff/fishermark/|title=Fisher, Mark, Goldsmiths, University of London|publisher=gold.ac.uk|access-date=2015-08-01|url-status=dead|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20150622122716/http://www.gold.ac.uk/visual-cultures/staff/fishermark/|archive-date=22 June 2015|df=dmy-all}}</ref> In 2009, Fisher edited ''The Resistible Demise of Michael Jackson'', a collection of critical essays on the career and death of [[Michael Jackson]], and published ''[[Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?]]'', an analysis of the ideological effects of [[neoliberalism]] on contemporary culture.

Fisher was an early critic of [[call-out culture]] and in 2013 published a controversial essay titled "[[Exiting the Vampire Castle]]".<ref>{{cite web |last=Fisher |first=Mark |title=Exiting the Vampire Castle |url=https://thenorthstar.info/2013/11/22/exiting-the-vampire-castle |date=22 November 2013 |archive-date=4 February 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180204081250/https://thenorthstar.info/2013/11/22/exiting-the-vampire-castle}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Exiting the Vampire Castle |url=https://www.opendemocracy.net/en/opendemocracyuk/exiting-vampire-castle/ |website=openDemocracy |access-date=2020-11-30}}</ref> He argued that call-out culture created a space "where solidarity is impossible, but guilt and fear are omnipresent". Fisher also argues that call-out culture reduces every political issue to criticizing the behaviour of individuals, instead of dealing with such political issues through collective action.<ref name="av">Vansintjan, Aaron. (29 October 2017) [https://www.opendemocracy.net/aaron-vansintjan/beyond-bloodsucking "Beyond Bloodsucking"], ''[[openDemocracy]]'', Retrieved 23 November 2018.</ref><ref>Izaakson, Jen. (12 August 2017)[https://www.feministcurrent.com/2017/08/12/kill-normies-skewers-online-identity-politics/ ‘Kill All Normies’ skewers online identity politics] ''[[Feminist Current]]''. Retrieved 23 November 2018.</ref> In 2014, Fisher published ''Ghosts of My Life: Writings on Depression, Hauntology and Lost Futures'', a collection of essays on similar themes viewed through the prisms of music, film, and [[hauntology]]. He also contributed intermittently to a number of publications, including ''[[Fact (UK magazine)|Fact]]'' and ''[[The Wire (magazine)|The Wire]]''. In 2016, Fisher co-edited a critical anthology on the post-punk era with [[Kodwo Eshun]] and [[Gavin Butt]] titled ''Post-Punk Then and Now'', published by [[Repeater Books]].<ref>Mankowski, Guy. "[http://www.3ammagazine.com/3am/post-punk-now-review/ ''Post-Punk Then and Now'': a review]", [[3:AM Magazine|''3:AM'' magazine]], 22 December 2016.</ref>

===Capitalist realism===
{{Main article|Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?}}
In the late 2000s, Fisher re-purposed the term "[[capitalist realism]]" to describe "the widespread sense that not only is capitalism the only viable political and economic system, but also that it is now impossible even to imagine a coherent alternative to it".<ref>{{Cite book|title=Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?|last=Fisher|first=Mark|publisher=[[Zero Books]]|year=2010|location=Winchester, UK|pages=2}}</ref>
He expanded on the concept in his 2009 book ''Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?'',<ref name=":0">Mark Fisher, ''Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?'' (Winchester, UK; Washington [D.C.]: Zero, 2009); {{ISBN|978-1-84694-317-1}} (pbk.); 1846943175 (pbk.).</ref> arguing that the term best describes the ideological situation since the fall of the [[Soviet Union]], in which the logics of capitalism have come to delineate the limits of political and social life, with significant effects on education, mental illness, pop culture, and methods of resistance.<ref name=":0" /> The result is a situation in which it is "easier to imagine an end to the world than an end to capitalism."<ref name=":6">{{Cite book|title=Capitalist realism : is there no alternative?|first=Mark|last=Fisher|date=2010-01-01|publisher=Zero Books|isbn=9781846943171|oclc=699737863}}</ref> Fisher writes:<ref>Mark Fisher, ''Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?'' (Winchester, UK; Washington [D.C.]: Zero, 2009).</ref>

<blockquote>Capitalist realism as I understand it ... is more like a pervasive atmosphere, conditioning not only the production of culture but also the regulation of work and education, and acting as a kind of invisible barrier constraining thought and action.</blockquote>

As a philosophical concept, capitalist realism is influenced by the [[Althusserian]] conception of ideology, as well as the work of [[Fredric Jameson]] and [[Slavoj Žižek]].<ref>{{Cite book|title=Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?|last=Fisher|first=Mark|publisher=Zero Books|year=2009|isbn=978-1846943171}}</ref> The concept of capitalist realism also likely stems from the concept of [[Cultural hegemony]] proposed by Italian theorist, [[Antonio Gramsci]]; which can generally be described as the notion that the "status quo" is all there is, and that anything else violates [[common sense]] itself. Capitalists maintain their power not through violence or force, but by creating a pervasive sense that the [[Capitalist]] system is all there is. They maintain this view by dominating most social and cultural institutions. Fisher proposes that within a capitalist framework there is no space to conceive of alternative forms of social structures, adding that younger generations are not even concerned with recognizing alternatives.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Capitalist Realism. Is There No Alternative?|last=Fisher|first=Mark|publisher=O Books|year=2009|isbn=9781846943171|pages=8}}</ref> He proposes that the [[Financial crisis of 2007–08|2008 financial crisis]] compounded this position; rather than catalyzing a desire to seek alternatives for the existing model, the response to the crisis reinforced the notion that modifications must be made within the existing system. Fisher argues that capitalist realism has propagated a 'business [[ontology]]' which concludes that everything should be run as a business including education and healthcare.<ref>{{cite book | first=Mark | last=Fisher | title=Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative? | location=Winchester, UK; Washington [D.C.] | publisher=Zero | year=2009 | page=15}}</ref>

Following the publication of Fisher's work, the term has been picked up by other literary critics.<ref>Prominently Mark Fisher and Jeremy Gilbert, 'Capitalist Realism and Neoliberal Hegemony: A Dialogue', ''New Formations'', 80—81 (2013), 89—101 DOI:10.3898/NEWF.80/81.05.2013; ''Reading Capitalist Realism'', ed. by Alison Shonkwiler and Leigh Claire La Berge (Iowa City: University of Iowa Press, 2014).</ref>

===Hauntology===
{{main|Hauntology|Hauntology (music)}}
[[File:Mark Fisher Slow Cancellation.png|thumb|Mark Fisher lecturing on the topic "The Slow Cancellation of the Future" in 2014]]
Fisher popularised the use of [[Jacques Derrida]]'s concept of [[hauntology]] to describe a pervasive sense in which contemporary culture is haunted by the "lost futures" of [[modernity]], which failed to occur or were cancelled by [[postmodernity]] and [[neoliberalism]].<ref name="ReferenceA">{{Cite web|url=https://dj.dancecult.net/index.php/dancecult/article/viewFile/378/391|title=Mark Fisher – The Metaphysics of Crackle: Afrofuturism and Hauntology}}</ref> Fisher and others have drawn attention to the shift into post-[[Fordist]] economies in the late 1970s, which he argued has "gradually and systematically deprived artists of the resources necessary to produce the new".<ref name="dj.dancecult.net">{{Cite web|url=https://dj.dancecult.net/index.php/dancecult/article/viewFile/378/391|title=The Metaphysics of Crackle: Afrofuturism and Hauntology}}</ref> In contrast to the [[nostalgia]] and ironic [[pastiche]] of postmodern culture, Fisher defined hauntological art as exploring these impasses and representing a "refusal to give up on the desire for the future" and a "pining for a future that never arrived".<ref name="sbe">{{cite book|title=William Basinski: Musician Snapshots|date=11 September 2015|publisher=SBE Media|pages=Chapter 3|last=Stone Blue Editors}}</ref><ref name="Fisher, Mark 2014">Fisher, Mark. ''Ghosts of My Life: Writings on Depression, Hauntology and Lost Futures''. [[Zero Books]], 30 May 2014. {{ISBN|978-1-78099-226-6}}</ref>{{page needed|date=October 2020}} Discussing the political relevance of the concept, Fisher wrote:<ref name="ReferenceA" /> <blockquote>At a time of [[Reactionary|political reaction]] and restoration, when cultural innovation has stalled and even gone backwards, when "power ... operates predictively as much as retrospectively" ([[Kodwo Eshun|Eshun]] 2003: 289), one function of hauntology is to keep insisting that there are futures beyond postmodernity's terminal time. When the present has given up on the future, we must listen for the relics of the future in the unactivated potentials of the past.</blockquote>
Fisher and critic [[Simon Reynolds]] adapted Derrida's concept to describe a [[Hauntology (music)|musical trend]] in the mid-2000s.<ref name="bloom">{{cite book |last=Albiez |first=Sean |title=Bloomsbury Encyclopedia of Popular Music of the World, Volume 11 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WKc0DwAAQBAJ |publisher=Bloomsbury |date=2017 |access-date=10 January 2020 |pages=347–349 |isbn=9781501326103}}</ref> Fisher's 2014 book ''Ghosts of My Life'' examined these ideas through cultural sources, such as the music of [[Burial (musician)|Burial]], [[Joy Division]], and the [[Ghost Box]] label, TV series such as ''[[Sapphire & Steel]]'', the films of [[Stanley Kubrick]] and [[Christopher Nolan]], and the novels of [[David Peace]] and [[John le Carré]].

===''The Weird and the Eerie''===
Fisher's posthumous book ''The Weird and the Eerie''<ref>{{cite web|url=https://repeaterbooks.com/product/the-weird-and-the-eerie/|title=The Weird and the Eerie {{!}} Repeater Books {{!}} Repeater Books|website=Repeater Books|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-07-16}}</ref> explores the titular concepts of "[[weird fiction|the weird]]" and "the eerie" through various works of art, defining the concepts as radical narrative modes or moments of "transcendental shock" which work to de-center the [[subject (philosophy)|human subject]]<ref>{{cite web |last=Daniel |first=James Rushing |title=The Weird and the Eerie |url=https://hkrbooks.com/2017/03/07/the-weird-and-the-eerie/ |website=Hong Kong Review of Books |date=7 March 2017 |access-date=28 March 2018}}</ref> and de-naturalise [[social reality]], exposing the arbitrary forces that shape it.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Woodard |first=Benjamin Graham |title=The Weird and the Eerie |journal=Textual Practice |volume=31 |issue=6 |pages=1181–1183 |date=2017 |doi=10.1080/0950236X.2017.1358704 |s2cid=149095699}}</ref> Summarizing Fisher's characterizations, Yohann Koshy stated that "weirdness abounds at the edge between worlds; eeriness radiates from the ruins of lost ones."<ref name="vice">{{cite web |last=Koshy |first=Yohann |title=The Revolution Will Be Weird and Eerie |url=https://www.vice.com/en_us/article/z4g4gj/the-revolution-will-be-weird-and-eerie |website=[[Vice (magazine)|Vice]] |date=20 February 2017 |access-date=28 February 2018}}</ref> The book includes discussion of [[science-fiction]] and [[horror fiction|horror]] sources such as the writing of [[H. P. Lovecraft]], [[Joan Lindsay]]'s 1967 ''[[Picnic at Hanging Rock (novel)|Picnic at Hanging Rock]]'', and [[Philip K. Dick]], films such as [[David Lynch]]'s ''[[Inland Empire (film)|Inland Empire]]'' (2006) and [[Jonathan Glazer]]'s ''[[Under the Skin (2013 film)|Under the Skin]]'' (2013), and the music of UK post-punk band [[The Fall (band)|The Fall]] and [[Ambient music|ambient]] musician [[Brian Eno]].<ref>{{cite web |last=Thacker |first=Eugene |title=Weird, Eerie, & Monstrous: Review of The Weird and the Eerie by Mark Fisher |url=http://www.boundary2.org/2017/06/eugene-thacker-weird-eerie-and-monstrous-a-review-of-the-weird-and-the-eerie-by-mark-fisher/ |website=boundary2 |date=27 June 2017}}</ref>

===''Acid Communism''===
At the time of his death, Fisher was said to be planning a new book titled ''Acid Communism'',<ref name="g"/> excerpts of which were published as part of a Mark Fisher anthology, ''k-punk: The Collected and Unpublished Writings of Mark Fisher (2004–2016)'', by [[Repeater Books]] in November 2018.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://thequietus.com/articles/23398-mark-fisher-acid-communism-anthology-release|title= Mark Fisher Anthology To Be Released|work=The Quietus|access-date=2017-10-18}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://repeaterbooks.com/product/k-punk-the-collected-and-unpublished-writings-of-mark-fisher-2004-2016/|title=k-punk: The Collected and Unpublished Writings of Mark Fisher (2004–2016) {{!}} Repeater Books {{!}} Repeater Books|website=Repeater Books|language=en-GB|access-date=2018-07-16}}</ref> ''Acid Communism'' would have attempted to reclaim elements of the [[1960s counterculture]] and [[psychedelia]] in the interest of imagining new political possibilities for [[Left-wing politics|the Left]].<ref name="g"/>

===''On Vanishing Land''===
Following Fisher's death, the Hyperdub record label started a sub label called Flatlines which published an audio-essay by Justin Barton and Fisher in July 2019. Fisher and Barton edited together music from various musicians which was made to accompany the text, and Barton, working in part with suggestions from Fisher, wrote the text for the audio-essay, which "evokes a walk along the Suffolk coastline in 2006, from Felixstowe container port ('a nerve ganglion of capitalism') to the Anglo-Saxon burial ground at Sutton Hoo". Both Barton and Fisher narrate the essay.<ref>{{cite web |title=On Vanishing Land, by Mark Fisher & Justin Barton |url=https://hyperdub.bandcamp.com/album/on-vanishing-land|access-date=2020-10-08|website=Hyperdub}}</ref> Adam Harper wrote about the elements of [[Hauntology (music)|Hauntology]] in ''On Vanishing Land'', as well as its relation to the environmentalist movement.<ref>{{cite web|title=Retracing Mark Fisher and Justin Barton's Eerie Pilgrimage {{!}} Frieze|url=https://www.frieze.com/article/retracing-mark-fisher-and-justin-bartons-eerie-pilgrimage-on-vanishing-land-hyperdub|access-date=2020-10-08|website=Frieze|date=23 July 2019|url-status=live|archive-url=https://archive.today/2021.03.05-003424/https://www.frieze.com/article/retracing-mark-fisher-and-justin-bartons-eerie-pilgrimage-on-vanishing-land-hyperdub|archive-date=2021-03-05}}</ref> In a review for ''[[The Quietus]]'', Johny Lamb referred to ''On Vanishing Land'' as a "shocking revelation of the proximity of dystopia."<ref>{{cite web|title=The Quietus {{!}} Features {{!}} The Lead Review {{!}} Into The Nerve Ganglion: Mark Fisher & Justin Barton On Vanishing Land|url=https://thequietus.com/articles/26849-mark-fisher-justin-barton-on-vanishing-land-review|first=Johny|last=Lamb|date=2019-07-25|access-date=2020-10-08|website=The Quietus|language=en-US|url-status=live|archive-date=2021-06-10|archive-url=https://archive.today/2021.06.10-055716/https://thequietus.com/articles/26849-mark-fisher-justin-barton-on-vanishing-land-review}}</ref>

=== Critique of political economy ===
Fisher [[Critique of political economy|critiqued economics]], claiming that it was a bourgeois "science", that molded reality after its presuppositions, rather than critically examined reality. As he stated it himself:

{{blockquote|From the start, “economy” was the object-cause of a bourgeois “science”, which hyperstitionally bootstrapped itself into existence, and then bent and melted the matter of this and every other world to fit its presuppositions — the greatest theocratic achievement in a history that was never human, an immense conjuring trick which works all the better because it came shrouded in that damp grey English and Scottish [[empiricism]] which claimed to have seen off all gods.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Fisher, Mark|url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/1023859141|title=K-punk : the collected and unpublished writings of Mark Fisher (2004-2016)|date=13 November 2018|isbn=978-1-912248-28-5|pages=620|oclc=1023859141}}</ref>
}}

==Death==
Fisher hanged himself at his home on King Street, [[Felixstowe]] on 13 January 2017 at the age of 48, shortly before the publication of his latest book ''The Weird and the Eerie'' (2017). He had sought psychiatric treatment in the weeks leading up to his death, but his [[general practitioner]] had only been able to offer over-the-phone meetings to discuss a referral. Fisher’s mental health had deteriorated since May 2016, leading to a suspected overdose in December 2016, where he was admitted to [[Ipswich Hospital]].<ref>"[https://www.ipswichstar.co.uk/news/renowned-writer-and-blogger-from-felixstowe-took-his-own-life-2787428 Renowned writer and K-Punk blogger Mark Fisher from Felixstowe took own life after battle with depression]", ''Ipswich Star'', 18 July 2017</ref> He discussed his struggles with [[Major depressive disorder|depression]] in articles<ref>E.g. "[https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2012/jul/16/mental-health-political-issue Why mental health is a political issue]" by Mark Fisher, ''The Guardian'', 16 July 2012</ref> and in his book ''Ghosts of My Life''. According to Simon Reynolds in ''[[The Guardian]]'', Fisher argued that "the pandemic of mental anguish that afflicts our time cannot be properly understood, or healed, if viewed as a private problem suffered by damaged individuals."<ref>{{cite web |last=Reynolds |first=Simon |title=Opinion: Mark Fisher's K-punk blogs were required reading for a generation |url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2017/jan/18/mark-fisher-k-punk-blogs-did-48-politics |work=The Guardian |date=18 January 2017}}</ref>

==Legacy==
Fisher has been posthumously acclaimed as a highly influential thinker and theorist.<ref>{{cite web |last=Seaton |first=Lola |title=The ghosts of Mark Fisher |url=https://www.newstatesman.com/mark-fisher-postcapitalist-desire-review |website=New Statesman |access-date=22 January 2021 |date=20 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last=Arcand |first=Rob |title=The Marxist Pop-Culture Theorist Who Influenced a Generation |url=https://www.thenation.com/article/archive/marxist-pop-culture-mark-fisher-kpunk-blog-review/ |website=The Nation |access-date=22 January 2021 |date=14 December 2018}}</ref> Commenting on Fisher's influence in ''[[Tribune magazine|Tribune]]'', Alex Niven recalled that Fisher's "lucidity, but more than that, his ability to get to the heart of what was wrong with late-capitalist culture and right about the putative alternative...seemed to have cracked some ineffable code".<ref>{{cite web |last=Niven |first=Alex |title=Our Debt to Mark Fisher |url=https://tribunemag.co.uk/2021/01/our-debt-to-mark-fisher |website=Tribune |access-date=22 January 2021 |date=13 January 2021}}</ref> In ''[[The Irish Times]]'' Rob Doyle wrote that "a more interesting British writer has not appeared in this century",<ref>{{cite web |last=Doyle |first=Rob |title=Is Mark Fisher this century's most interesting British writer? |url=https://www.irishtimes.com/culture/is-mark-fisher-this-century-s-most-interesting-british-writer-1.3835122 |website=The Irish Times |access-date=22 January 2021 |date=30 March 2019}}</ref> while ''[[The Guardian]]'' described Fisher's k-punk blog posts as "required reading for a generation".<ref>{{cite web |last=Reynolds |first=Simon |title=Mark Fisher's K-punk blogs were required reading for a generation |url=https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2017/jan/18/mark-fisher-k-punk-blogs-did-48-politics |website=The Guardian |access-date=22 January 2021 |date=19 January 2017}}</ref> In the ''[[Los Angeles Review of Books]]'', Roger Luckhurst called Fisher "one of Britain's most trenchant, clear-sighted, and sparky cultural commentators...it is a catastrophe that we no longer have Mark Fisher".<ref>{{cite web |last=Luckhurst |first=Roger |title=The Necessity of Being Judgmental: On "k-punk: The Collected and Unpublished Writings of Mark Fisher" |url=https://lareviewofbooks.org/article/the-necessity-of-being-judgmental-on-k-punk-the-collected-and-unpublished-writings-of-mark-fisher |website=Los Angeles Review of Books |access-date=22 January 2021 |date=9 March 2019}}</ref> He still has a large influence on contemporary Zer0 Books writers, with him being cited extensively in [[Guy Mankowski]]'s 'Albion's Secret History: Snapshots of England's Pop Rebels and Outsiders'.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bvNCR3_HWZU|title=Remembering a Time Before the Great Culture War |date=11 January 2018 |access-date=8 March 2021 |website=Zer0 Books Youtube Channel|last=Mankowski |first=Guy |url-status=live}}</ref> After Fisher's suicide, English musician [[the Caretaker (musician)|the Caretaker]], who had a symbiotic relationship with the writer,<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://thequietus.com/articles/23832-take-care-it-s-a-desert-out-there-in-memory-of-and-for-mark-fisher-the-caretaker |title=Remembering Mark Fisher With The Caretaker's "Take Care. It's A Desert Out There..." |date=11 January 2018 |access-date=11 May 2021 |website=The Quietus |last=Scovell |first=Adam |url-status=live}}</ref> released ''[[Take Care. It's a Desert Out There...]]'' in memory of him, with its proceeds being donated to the mental health charity [[Mind (charity)|Mind]].<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.thewire.co.uk/news/51904/the-caretaker-and-boomkat-donate-proceeds-from-take-care-it-sa-desert-out-there-in-memory- |title=The Caretaker and Boomkat donate proceeds from Take Care, It's A Desert Out There in memory of Mark Fisher |date=25 July 2018 |access-date=11 May 2021 |website=[[The Wire (magazine)|The Wire]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180725175920/https://www.thewire.co.uk/news/51904/the-caretaker-and-boomkat-donate-proceeds-from-take-care-it-sa-desert-out-there-in-memory- |archive-date=25 July 2018 |url-status=live}}</ref>

==Bibliography==
* ''The Resistible Demise of Michael Jackson'' (editor). Winchester: Zero Books, 2009. {{ISBN|978-1846943485}}
* ''[[Capitalist Realism: Is There No Alternative?]]''. Winchester: Zero Books, 2009. {{ISBN|978-1846943171}}
* ''Ghosts of My Life: Writings on Depression, Hauntology and Lost Futures''. Winchester: Zero Books, 2014. {{ISBN|978-1780992266}}
* ''Post-Punk Then and Now'' (editor, with [[Gavin Butt]] and [[Kodwo Eshun]]). London: [[Repeater Books]], 2016. {{ISBN|978-1910924266|}}
* ''The Weird and the Eerie''. London: Repeater Books, 2017. {{ISBN|978-1910924389}}
* ''Flatline Constructs: Gothic Materialism and Cybernetic Theory-Fiction'' (foreword by exmilitary). New York: Exmilitary Press, 2018. {{ISBN|978-0692066058}}
* ''k-punk: The Collected and Unpublished Writings of Mark Fisher (2004–2016)'' (edited by Darren Ambrose, foreword by [[Simon Reynolds]]). London: Repeater Books, 2018. {{ISBN|978-1912248292|}}
* ''Postcapitalist Desire: The Final Lectures'' (edited and with an introduction by Matt Colquhoun). London: Repeater Books, 2020. {{ISBN|9781913462482}}

==References==
{{reflist}}

==External links==
* {{cite web |title=An Extract From Mark Fisher's Ghosts Of My Life |url=http://thequietus.com/articles/13004-mark-fisher-ghosts-of-my-life-extract |publisher=thequietus.com |access-date=2015-08-01}}
* [http://rwm.macba.cat/en/sonia/sonia148_mark_fisher/capsula 2012 podcast discussion with Mark Fisher]&nbsp;– discussing issues relative to the recession, insurrection, and Really Existing Capitalism
* [http://k-punk.abstractdynamics.org/ K-Punk Blog Archive]&nbsp;– Mark Fishers "K-Punk" Blog
* [https://markfishercyberfield.github.io/ Mark Fisher Tribute Site & Video Archive]
* [https://dissensus.com Dissensus forum]

{{Hauntology}}{{Critique of political economy}}{{Continental philosophy}}{{Authority control}}

{{DEFAULTSORT:Fisher, Mark}}
[[Category:1968 births]]
[[Category:2017 suicides]]
[[Category:Suicides by hanging in England]]
[[Category:English music journalists]]
[[Category:English Marxists]]
[[Category:British literary theorists]]
[[Category:English bloggers]]
[[Category:Alumni of the University of Hull]]
[[Category:Alumni of the University of Warwick]]
[[Category:British anti-capitalists]]
[[Category:Social critics]]
[[Category:Academics of Goldsmiths, University of London]]
[[Category:20th-century British journalists]]
[[Category:21st-century British journalists]]
[[Category:English political philosophers]]
[[Category:British male bloggers]]
[[Category:Critics of political economy]]
[[Category:Accelerationism]]
[[Category:Mark Fisher]][[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Oldest pages before the refresh]]
[[Category:Pages]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
